sections.markdown:
# Quote: Code as Data

By default the evaluator treats every list as a function call. To say "this list is just data, don't evaluate it," use **quote**:

```lisp
(quote (1 2 3))   ; the list (1 2 3)
```

Quote is so common it has reader-syntax: `'X` is read as `(quote X)`. So `'(1 2 3)` and `(quote (1 2 3))` are identical.

Quote also lets you refer to **symbols themselves** instead of their values. `'foo` is the symbol named FOO; without the quote, Lisp would try to look up FOO's value.

Quoting is *shallow*: subforms inside a quoted list are also unevaluated.

sections.explain: Returns the literal list (1 2 3), no function call attempted.
The symbol FOO (printed in uppercase — symbols are case-folded by the reader).
Inside a quote, nothing is evaluated. The result is a 3-element list.
The first element of the unevaluated list is the symbol +.
We pass three quoted symbols to LIST. LIST evaluates each — and a quoted symbol evaluates to the symbol itself.

sections.prompt: Define HEAD-SYMBOL that, given a non-empty list whose first element is a symbol, returns that symbol.
Define ARITY that returns how many arguments a quoted call expression has — the length minus one (the operator).
